name: Bijan Robinson
blurb: Update: We don't often get a top-10 running back selection in the NFL Draft, but we did this year when the Falcons drafted Bijan Robinson. His pre- to post-draft percentile ranking didn't change a ton, but the landing spot could've been a lot worse than Atlanta. Falcons running backs low-key crushed it last year. Robinson's Draft Capital Delta isn't huge because early-round picks won't have big numbers within the statistic. His prospect score places him elite company, though. He's the top pick in rookie drafts this year.
source_quote: 1   Bijan Robinson           RB     1     1   Bijan Robinson          RB     1

name: Jahmyr Gibbs
blurb: Update: The Lions snagged Jahmyr Gibbs much earlier than expected, and it gave his prospect score a huge boost. The main concern with Gibbs is his size, and that hasn't changed. Gibbs also landed on a team that's likely looking to divvy up the backfield. With all this being said, it's clear that Gibbs is a unique, outlier-type prospect. He joins Saquon Barkley and Christian McCaffrey as the only first-round running backs since 2011 with a best-season reception share above 17%. Gibbs is an elite pass-catcher with crazy speed.
source_quote: 2   Jahmyr Gibbs             RB     2     2   Anthony Richardson      QB     2

name: Jaxon Smith-Njigba
blurb: Update: Pre-draft, Jaxon Smith-Njigba was projected to go around 12th overall. He fell to 20th, and he landed in a spot — Seattle — where he may struggle to put up top-end production right away. This is dynasty, though. Smith-Njigba ran 89% of his routes from the slot during his monster Sophomore season at Ohio State, and playing alongside DK Metcalf will allow JSN to continue to see a lot of snaps there. There's an outcome here where, eventually, JSN is seeing more work in this offense than Metcalf.
source_quote: 3   Jaxon Smith-Njigba       WR     3     3   Bryce Young             QB     2

name: Jordan Addison
blurb: Update: The Jordan Addison-Minnesota Vikings pair is perfect. He's not likely to be an alpha wideout in the NFL, but playing alongside Justin Jefferson means he won't have to be. Without Adam Thielen in the mix, Addison should be able to get reps both on the outside and the inside as the team's number-two wide receiver option while seeing easier coverage. When looking at 2023 projections, it's clear that Addison could perform best among any wide receiver in this class. If you're looking for a pretty safe bet at wide receiver, Addison's your guy.
source_quote: 4   Jordan Addison           WR     4     4   CJ Stroud               QB     2

name: Quentin Johnston
blurb: Update: There's a lot of volatility to Quentin Johnston's analytical profile, but at least the landing spot is secure. The biggest issue with Johnston is that he's a physically gifted wide receiver who tends to play a little small. His best-season yards per team pass attempt of 2.35 is merely average, but had he stayed healthy during his Freshman and Sophomore seasons, he had a chance to hit the 2.00 mark within that metric during all three collegiate seasons. Drafting Johnston depends on your risk tolerance.
source_quote: 5   Quentin Johnston         WR     4     5   Jahmyr Gibbs            RB     3

name: Zay Flowers
blurb: Update: Zay Flowers' comps are rough, and it's because he's a non-early-declare wide receiver who had good-not-great collegiate production. But when you dig a little deeper, you start to uncover things that point to him being a really good player. Flowers, despite his size, largely played a perimeter role in college. Flowers is now paired up with Lamar Jackson in Baltimore. Historically, that hasn't been a great match for a wide receiver. He's still a fine enough rookie pick. The talent is there.
source_quote: 6   Zay Flowers              WR     4     6   Jaxon Smith-Njigba      WR     4

name: Anthony Richardson
blurb: Anthony Richardson may not have been the first quarterback drafted, but with Shane Steichen and his athletic profile, he has the highest ceiling at quarterback in this class.
source_quote: 7   Anthony Richardson       QB     5     7   Jordan Addison          WR     5

name: Zach Charbonnet
blurb: Update: The draft capital was there for Zach Charbonnet. The landing spot was not. Pete Carroll loves himself some running backs, and for a second straight year, Seattle took a back with a second-round pick. Charbonnet has the rare combination of size and a good receiving profile. Clearly, the problem here is that Charbonnet is now sharing a backfield with Kenneth Walker. That's where Charbonnet can help. It's certainly a rough landing spot for Charbonnet. We're unlikely going to see him become a high-end fantasy back without a Walker injury.
source_quote: 8   Zach Charbonnet          RB     5     8   Quentin Johnston        WR     5

name: Devon Achane
blurb: Update: There was one obvious landing spot for Devon Achane in this year's draft that would've made him a much more attractive fantasy option. And he landed there. Going to Mike McDaniel and the Dolphins is perfect. The size thing is still an issue, of course. Achane is only 188 pounds. But of the 19 running backs in the prospect database who were under that 190-pound mark prior to this year's draft, none were top-100 picks like Achane. Achane might not see a bell-cow workload, but he has a chance to be insanely efficient.
source_quote: 9   Devon Achane             RB     5     9   Zay Flowers             WR     5

name: Dalton Kincaid
blurb: There's a new tight end model that's not quite ready to be released, and it likes Sam LaPorta most in this class. Had Dalton Kincaid tested, it would've been close. Kincaid gets the TE1 spot because of landing spot, but LaPorta is legit.
source_quote: 10   Dalton Kincaid           TE     5    10   Will Levis              QB     6

name: Bryce Young
blurb: The tiebreaker being used for CJ Stroud versus Bryce Young is draft capital.
source_quote: 11   Bryce Young              QB     6    11   Zach Charbonnet         RB     6

name: CJ Stroud
blurb: The tiebreaker being used for CJ Stroud versus Bryce Young is draft capital.
source_quote: 12   CJ Stroud                QB     6    12   Devon Achane            RB     6

name: Jayden Reed
blurb: Update: My favorite wide receiver sleeper in this year's draft is a sleeper no more. With the 50th overall pick, the Green Bay Packers selected Jayden Reed out of Michigan State. Reed played all over the field when he got to Michigan State, and he got work as a returner as well. His production profile doesn't look elite in the Z-Prospect Model, per se, but the production is definitely there. Reed shouldn't have trouble seeing some action as a rookie for the Packers.
source_quote: 13   Jayden Reed              WR     6    13   Dalton Kincaid          TE     6

name: Marvin Mims
blurb: Update: Marvin Mims was drafted in the second round by the Denver Broncos, and his breakout happened before he turned 19. Mims is an easy prospect to like. He's a big play waiting to happen after seeing average depth of targets of 17.0, 17.6, and 15.4 during his three collegiate seasons. He did that while running his routes all over the field — despite his size, he wasn't locked into the slot. He's an early-declare wideout in a class that doesn't have a ton of them. Even if we can't currently see a super clear path to relevancy, in dynasty, you want to target good players.
source_quote: 14   Marvin Mims              WR     6    14   Jayden Reed             WR     7

name: Kendre Miller
blurb: Update: The New Orleans Saints backfield might appear to be crowded — and it currently is — but that doesn't mean it will be forever. Kendre Miller, the backfield's newest member, is one of two backs in this year's class who has yet to turn 21. Miller has the right size to be a bell-cow, but his collegiate receiving profile was lacking. So the missing receiving production is a problem. The three-down skill-set may not be obvious, but Miller's at least great on the ground.
source_quote: 15   Kendre Miller            RB     6    15   Marvin Mims             WR     7

name: Jonathan Mingo
blurb: Update: When this prospect guide initially launched, Jonathan Mingo was projected to go 170th overall in this year's draft. And then he went 39th to the Carolina Panthers. Mingo's analytical profile is nothing special. He's not an early-declare pass-catcher, and his top season in yards per team pass attempt was just 2.22. The biggest reason Mingo rose in the draft — more than likely — is his size. Mingo isn't someone the fantasy community likes given his poor analytical profile. But that's why we use draft capital.
source_quote: 16   Jonathan Mingo           WR     6    16   Kendre Miller           RB     7

name: Sam LaPorta
blurb: There's a new tight end model that's not quite ready to be released, and it likes Sam LaPorta most in this class. Had Dalton Kincaid tested, it would've been close. Kincaid gets the TE1 spot because of landing spot, but LaPorta is legit.
source_quote: 17   Sam LaPorta              TE     6    17   Jonathan Mingo          WR     7

name: Josh Downs
blurb: Update: The Colts got a steal with Josh Downs. At least, that's what the Z-Prospect Model thinks. Downs has the highest best-season yards per team pass attempt rate in this class at 3.52. He joins Tyler Lockett as the only third-round wide receiver since 2011 to have a best-season yards per team pass attempt rate above 3.50 and a Breakout Age earlier than 20 years old. Indianapolis isn't the best destination in the world for Downs. But we've got to trust the process here. Downs' prospect score is strong.
source_quote: 18   Josh Downs               WR     7    18   Sam LaPorta             TE     7

name: Jalin Hyatt
blurb: Update: Jalin Hyatt fell a lot further in the draft than most expected, but now that he's a third-rounder, it's easier to be bullish on him versus expectation. Hyatt's analytical profile is kind of all over the place. He saw very little production during his first two seasons at Tennessee, and then he blew up in 2022. Hyatt's more than likely going to be asked to play the perimeter in the NFL in order to stretch defenses vertically with his speed. He still has potential to be a fantasy contributor in an offense that lacks high-end weapons.
source_quote: 19   Jalin Hyatt              WR     7    19   Hendon Hooker           QB     7

name: Roschon Johnson
blurb: Update: Roschon Johnson's collegiate production profile sucks because he played with one of the best running back prospects of the decade. There are some quality pieces to Johnson's profile anyway. He had a passable best-season reception share of 7.5%, and he did that at 219 pounds. Johnson was drafted by Chicago with the 115th overall pick, and it's not a terrible spot for a running back to go. Johnson has a shot to prove himself worthy of that role. Expecting this backfield to not be a committee is probably foolish, though.
source_quote: 20   Roschon Johnson          RB     8    20   Josh Downs              WR     8

name: Rashee Rice
blurb: Update: You have to imagine Rashee Rice got a lot pricier in rookie drafts when he was selected by the Chiefs in the second round. The Z-Prospect Model isn't super excited. Now, to be fair, the model doesn't have some Patrick Mahomes input. But we've learned to not ignore the prospect himself just because he's playing with the greatest young quarterback of all time. Rice has good size, and his stat score is a top-10 one in this year's class. He's not a bad prospect, but he's not someone with incredible odds to hit, either.
source_quote: 21   Rashee Rice              WR     8    21   Jalin Hyatt             WR     8

name: Michael Mayer
blurb: Luke Musgrave is great in the new model, too. It likes him more than Michael Mayer thanks to better athleticism. That's why they're in the same tier.
source_quote: 22   Michael Mayer            TE     8    22   Roschon Johnson         RB     9

name: Tank Bigsby
blurb: Update: Tank Bigsby's size and best-season reception share isn't easy to find. There've only been eight running backs since 2011 to have a best-season reception share at or above 18% while weighing in at 210-plus pounds. Bigsby makes four. Unfortunately, Bigsby fell to Jacksonville where he'll be competing for touches with Travis Etienne. Bigsby's got a really solid prospect score, but he's going to be playing with Etienne for at least the next two seasons. He'll be a flex play at best with the chance to step into a starter role with an injury.
source_quote: 23   Tank Bigsby              RB     9    23   Rashee Rice             WR     9

name: Tyjae Spears
blurb: Update: The best non-Power Five running back in this year's class got selected in the third round by the Tennessee Titans. He's not Derrick Henry, but Tyjae Spears is also not a bad prospect. The reason why the Z-Prospect Model doesn't like Spears more is because efficiency isn't what drives the model. Tennessee isn't a bad landing spot, though. Given the lack of touches to his profile and his 201-pound frame, it doesn't seem likely that Spears is going to dominate looks in the NFL. Could he be a really good committee back? Absolutely.
source_quote: 24   Tyjae Spears             RB     9    24   Michael Mayer           TE     9

name: Luke Musgrave
blurb: Luke Musgrave is great in the new model, too. It likes him more than Michael Mayer thanks to better athleticism. That's why they're in the same tier.
source_quote: 25   Luke Musgrave            TE     9    25   Tank Bigsby             RB    10

name: Cedric Tillman
blurb: Update: The Z-Prospect Model isn't the biggest Cedric Tillman fan. He's got great size at 6'3'', 213 pounds, but his Breakout Age is one of the worst in the class among players who broke out, and he's entering the league at 23 years old. Tillman had an impressive Junior season with Tennessee, and he hit a 2.89 yards per team pass attempt rate. The Browns were the team who grabbed Tillman in the third. That's sort of the problem for Tillman. It'd be one thing to wait it out with a raw prospect who was 21 years old, but he's an older player entering the league.
source_quote: 26   Cedric Tillman           WR     9    26   Tyjae Spears            RB    10

name: Israel Abanikanda
blurb: Update: There was hope that Izzy Abanikanda would get drafted on Day 2, but he ended up getting snatched in the fifth round instead. To the Jets. With Breece Hall there, it's not a great spot for fantasy production. And it's unfortunate, because Abanikanda has the goods. He's a bigger back at 216 pounds, and he had an impressive Speed Score of 109. He also is the youngest running back in this year's class. Abanikanda had a chance to be a massive sleeper in this year's class. It's a pretty disappointing landing spot when you consider what could've been.
source_quote: 27   Israel Abanikanda        RB    10    27   Luke Musgrave           TE    10

name: Chase Brown
blurb: Update: Chase Brown once had high projected draft capital, but things slowly got worse and worse as March and April moved along. He ended up going in Round 5, giving him a pretty mid prospect score. But he got drafted by Cincinnati, and that's mega intriguing. Brown has some juice. He ran a 4.43 at the NFL Combine, finishing with the fourth-best Speed Score in this year's class. This is probably one of the best Day 3 situations overall, though. Brown is a good rookie draft target, assuming his cost doesn't get out of hand.
source_quote: 28   Chase Brown              RB    10    28   Cedric Tillman          WR    10

name: Kayshon Boutte
blurb: Update: This may be a little galaxy brained, but Kayshon Boutte going to New England might be the best thing possible from a fantasy perspective. His rookie draft cost has been slipping since his awful showing at the combine, and now he's a Day 3 pick. If Boutte becomes a last-round dart throw, he's the perfect target. And, I mean, objectively, Boutte's profile isn't horrible. There's a ton — a ton — of red flags surrounding Boutte. You shouldn't be giving up a top-two round rookie pick to acquire him in any format. With that being said, he's someone to keep an eye on.
source_quote: 29   Kayshon Boutte           WR    10    29   Israel Abanikanda       RB    11

name: Tank Dell
blurb: Update: Nathaniel "Tank" Dell goes from Houston to…Houston. The Texans snagged the small wideout in the third round, and Dell officially became just the fourth sub-170-pound wide receiver drafted in the top-100 since 2011. On the plus side, Dell has the second-best stat score within this draft class, trailing only Josh Downs. On the minus side, he didn't have a super strong Breakout Age. Dell's a solid player, but he may lack the upside we want from a fantasy contributor.
source_quote: 30   Tank Dell                WR    10    30   Chase Brown             RB    11

name: Tyler Scott
blurb: Update: It was odd to see Tyler Scott get picked 33 spots after his Cincinnati teammate, Tre Tucker, but the Bears got a good one at the tail-end of the fourth round of this year's draft. Scott had a best-season yards per team pass attempt rate of only 2.17, but he hit a max-season touchdown share of almost 41%, one of the better marks in the class. He's got great speed to stretch the field and profiles best, given his size, as a vertical slot threat in the NFL. He's a pretty good later-round option in rookie drafts.
source_quote: 31   Tyler Scott              WR    10    31   Kayshon Boutte          WR    11

name: Tucker Kraft
source_quote: 32   Tucker Kraft             TE    11    32   Tank Dell               WR    11

name: Trey Palmer
blurb: There's only one wide receiver in this year's draft class with a best-season yards per team pass attempt rate of 3.00 or better to go along with a best-season touchdown share mark above 50%. And his name is Trey Palmer. The problem with Palmer's numbers is that he did very little across the first three years of his college career. He was at LSU during that time. Palmer made the most of his opportunity in 2022, at least, but the late-career production gave him a poor Breakout Age. Palmer's capable of creating big plays down the field.
source_quote: 33   Trey Palmer              WR    11    33   Tyler Scott             WR    11

name: Puka Nacua
blurb: Update: Among all 50-plus target wide receivers last season, Puka Nacua ranked second in yards per route run. He didn't score very well in best-season yards per team pass attempt (2.03), but that's partially due to the fact that he only played 9 of 13 games. Nacua started his college career with Washington before transferring to Brigham Young as a Junior in 2021. Nacua ended up falling to the Rams in Round 5. The Z-Prospect Model doesn't have high hopes for Nacua, but there are reasons to feel OK about his outlook.
source_quote: 34   Puka Nacua               WR    11    34   Tucker Kraft            TE    12

name: Zach Evans
blurb: Update: The Z-Prospect Model and Zach Evans don't get along very well. All three of his production metrics in the model are well below average, and he weighed in at just 202 pounds at the combine. To be fair to Evans, he did play alongside strong talent in college, and he wasn't always able to stay healthy. Going to the Rams could offer up some rookie-season opportunity for Evans. If he shows up, Cam Akers is a free agent next year, so Evans could hypothetically walk into the 2024 season as LA's starter.
source_quote: 35   Zach Evans               RB    12    35   Trey Palmer             WR    12

name: Darnell Washington
source_quote: 36   Darnell Washington       TE    12    36   Puka Nacua              WR    12

name: DeWayne McBride
blurb: Update: One of the better running back landing spots in this year's draft was Minnesota, and they decided to take a chance with DeWayne McBride in Round 7. What I do know is that McBride has one glaring weakness as a prospect, and that's pass-catching ability. He has one of the worst best-season reception shares in the database at 1.7%. No running back had that low of a share with a best-season total yards per team play rate above 2.0, though. McBride was ultra-productive on the ground. Could he maybe be a Damien Harris-type back for the Vikings? It's possible.
source_quote: 37   DeWayne McBride          RB    12    37   Zach Evans              RB    13

name: Deuce Vaughn
blurb: Update: There's no denying Deuce Vaughn's production profile. His stat score isn't only the best in this year's draft class, but it's the best in the entire Z-Prospect database. Vaughn was that dude at Kansas State. The problem? Well, with the pre-draft version of this guide, the problem was only his size. He's 5'5'', matching JJ Taylor as the shortest back to either get drafted or go to the NFL Combine since 2011. He's merely a low-ceiling, medium-floor late-round rookie pick now.
source_quote: 38   Deuce Vaughn             RB    12    38   Darnell Washington      TE    13

name: Evan Hull
blurb: Update: Before this year's draft, the model's database had nine running backs with a 20% best-season reception share or better. Christian McCaffrey, James White, and Tony Pollard were all hits, and Rachaad White's in that group as well. Evan Hull is part of that group now. Hull is a pass-catching back with good size and athleticism. You can see that the model likes him, but, unfortunately, the landing spot couldn't be much worse. He fell to Indianapolis at the end of the fifth round. Had Hull gone somewhere interesting, he'd be a priority in rookie drafts.
source_quote: 39   Evan Hull                RB    13    39   Stetson Bennett         QB    13

name: Luke Schoonmaker
source_quote: 40   Luke Schoonmaker         TE    13    40   DeWayne McBride         RB    13

name: Michael Wilson
blurb: Update: There are Michael Wilson truthers out there, but I — and the model — am not one of them. Wilson has the right frame for an NFL wide receiver, but he struggled mightily to stay healthy during his time at Stanford. That resulted in a best-season yards per team play rate of 1.51, one of the lower rates in the class. He also didn't test exceptionally well as an athlete, making the Day 2 selection by Arizona that much more surprising. The Cardinals are going through some changes, so there's opportunity for him to show up and get some work.
source_quote: 41   Michael Wilson           WR    13    41   Deuce Vaughn            RB    13

name: AT Perry
blurb: AT Perry is 6'4'', but it doesn't really matter. Age-adjusted production is what's key, and Perry doesn't exactly have it. His best seasons in yards per team pass attempt and touchdown share are below average, and he broke out late in college. He redshirted his Freshman year, and it took him three seasons at Wake Forest to post substantial production. He enters the draft well over 23 years of age, too. This type of profile — AT Perry's profile — is volatile. Could the height translate? Of course. Is it something you should bank on? Probably not.
source_quote: 42   AT Perry                 WR    13    42   Evan Hull               RB    14

name: Eric Gray
blurb: Update: Eric Gray got some love in this guide pre-draft, and then he tested at his Pro Day. He ran a 4.62 40-yard dash, giving him a Pro Day-adjusted Speed Score south of 90. Gray going to the Giants is pretty interesting. The G-Men don't have anyone of significance behind Saquon Barkley on the depth chart, and Barkley himself may not be around in 2024. So Gray has a shot to appreciate in value from 2023 to 2024 if things break right. Strictly as a prospect, though, there just doesn't seem to be enough upside to Gray's profile.
source_quote: 43   Eric Gray                RB    14    43   Luke Schoonmaker        TE    14

name: Xavier Hutchinson
blurb: Biletnikoff Award finalists are typically going to have strong production profiles. And Xavier Hutchinson, a finalist last season, caught 107 passes for 1,171 yards and 6 scores in 2022. Those numbers look good on paper, but they're not as impressive as you'd think. Hutchinson is fine in the Z-Prospect Model on the production end, but his yards per team pass attempt and touchdown share marks were ordinary. Hutchinson enters the NFL Draft as an older player — he'll be almost 23 — but his Breakout Age was OK.
source_quote: 44   Xavier Hutchinson        WR    14    44   Michael Wilson          WR    14

name: Charlie Jones
blurb: Update: It's easy to poke fun at Charlie Jones' profile. He started his collegiate career all the way back in 2018 with Buffalo before transferring to Iowa. He spent two years there without much production before moving to Purdue, where he played his final season. And that final season was pretty good. He caught 110 passes for 1,361 yards. With all that movement, Jones is unsurprisingly one of the oldest wide receivers in this class at 24 and a half. So stashing Jones with a last-round pick isn't a bad idea.
source_quote: 45   Charlie Jones            WR    14    45   AT Perry                WR    14

name: Brenton Strange
source_quote: 46   Brenton Strange          TE    14    46   Eric Gray               RB    15

name: Parker Washington
blurb: Update: Parker Washington's prospect score makes him look better than he probably is. He's built more like a running back than a wide receiver, and he largely played the slot during his three seasons at Penn State. Washington's production was never elite, but it was good enough to give him a strong Breakout Age. There's very little chance he's a high-volume player on Jacksonville in 2023 with Christian Kirk, Calvin Ridley, and Zay Jones in the mix. All this means is that Washington is a stash more than anything.
source_quote: 47   Parker Washington        WR    14    47   Xavier Hutchinson       WR    15

name: Dontayvion Wicks
blurb: Dontayvion Wicks missed his Sophomore year with an injury sustained before the season started, but he bounced back in a big way the following year, posting the ninth-highest yards per route run rate in college football among 50-plus target wide receivers. That 2021 campaign was by far his best, but within the Z-Prospect Model, it's not looking special: All three of his production metrics clocked in below average. It's not looking likely that Wicks is going to be an every-week starter in your fantasy lineup on Sundays.
source_quote: 48   Dontayvion Wicks         WR    15    48   Charlie Jones           WR    15

name: Hendon Hooker
source_quote: 19   Hendon Hooker           QB     7

name: Will Levis
source_quote: 10   Will Levis              QB     6

name: Stetson Bennett
source_quote: 39   Stetson Bennett         QB    13
